coredata.dc:title: β-Cyclodextrin functionalized molybdenum disulfide quantum dots as nanoprobe for sensitive fluorescent detection of parathion-methyl
coredata.dc:creator: Yi, Yinhui
Zeng, Wei
Zhu, Gangbing
coredata.dc:description: Through modifying molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (MoS2 QDs) with 3-aminophenyl boronic acid and functionalizing further with hydropropyl-β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), a novel nanoprobe based on β-CD functionalized MoS2 QDs (β-CD-MoS2 QDs) was developed for the fluorescent detection of parathion-methyl (MP). β-CD-MoS2 QDs was characterized with various technologies including transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra. As for MP detection, MP was hydrolyzed to p-nitrophenol (p-NP) under the alkaline conditions, and p-NP can enter into the β-CD cavity due to the host-guest recognition capability of β-CD, which then results the fluorescence quenching of nanoprobe. Based on this principle, an enzyme-free fluorescence sensing platform were constructed for MP. Under the optimal conditions, β-CD-MoS2 QDs nanoprobe exhibits wide detection scope (0.01–18.0 ppm) and low detection limits (3.3 ppb) for MP detection. In addition, the nanoprobe has excellent selectivity for MP, and it can be applied to detect MP in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Parathion-methyl
Quantum dots
Cyclodextrin
Fluorescent sensing
Organophosphorus pesticides
Probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of MP by β-CD-MoS2 QDs
PET process between β-CD-MoS2 QDs and p-NP
Fluorescence detection of MP based on β-CD-MoS2 QDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image and size distribution of β-CD-MoS2 QDs; (B) the XPS spectrum of β-CD-MoS2 QDs.
Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of β-CD-MoS2 QDs (solid line) and MoS2 QDs (dashed line).

(A) The fluorescence spectra of β-CD-MoS2 QDs in the presence of different concentration of p-NP (0.0–12.00 μM); (B) time-resolved decay fitting line of β-CD-MoS2 QDs in the absence and presence of p-NP (100 μM).

(A) The fluorescence spectra of mere MoS2 QDs in the absence (black line) and presence (red line) of hydrolyzed MP (80.0 ppm); (B) the fluorescence spectra of β-CD-MoS2 in the presence of MP (160.0 ppm) and hydrolyzed MP (80 ppm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) The fluorescence spectra of β-CD-MoS2 with increasing concentration of MP in the range of 0.0–36.0 ppm; (B) the linear relationship between (F0–F)/F0 and MP concentration. (Where F0 represents the fluorescence of β-CD-MoS2 without MP, and F represents the fluorescence of β-CD-MoS2 with different MP concentration).
Schematic illustrations for (A) MP hydrolysis and (B) fluorescent sensing MP based on β-CD-MoS2 QDs nanoprobe through host-guest recognition.

head.ce:title: β-Cyclodextrin functionalized molybdenum disulfide quantum dots as nanoprobe for sensitive fluorescent detection of parathion-methyl

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Through modifying molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (MoS2 QDs) with 3-aminophenyl boronic acid and functionalizing further with hydropropyl-β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), a novel nanoprobe based on β-CD functionalized MoS2 QDs (β-CD-MoS2 QDs) was developed for the fluorescent detection of parathion-methyl (MP). β-CD-MoS2 QDs was characterized with various technologies including transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra. As for MP detection, MP was hydrolyzed to p-nitrophenol (p-NP) under the alkaline conditions, and p-NP can enter into the β-CD cavity due to the host-guest recognition capability of β-CD, which then results the fluorescence quenching of nanoprobe. Based on this principle, an enzyme-free fluorescence sensing platform were constructed for MP. Under the optimal conditions, β-CD-MoS2 QDs nanoprobe exhibits wide detection scope (0.01–18.0 ppm) and low detection limits (3.3 ppb) for MP detection. In addition, the nanoprobe has excellent selectivity for MP, and it can be applied to detect MP in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are a cluster of organic pesticides which have been extensively utilized in agriculture due to the excellent ability to control pests and plant diseases [1–3]. The residues substances of OPs have been found to cause organ dysfunction, cancer disease and fetal anomaly even at very low concentration. Parathion-methyl (MP) is a typical OPs which incline to accumulate in food and water because of its bioaccumulation and low solubility [4]. According to the guidelines for drinking water quality ruled by World Health Organization, the concentration of MP in agricultural water ranges up to 1.7 nM increasing during summer months. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) also regulates that the safe level of MP in drinking water is as follows: 0.11 μM for longer term exposure of children, 1.1 μM for 1 d or 10 d of exposure for children; and 7.6 nM for the adults lifetime exposure [5,6]. Therefore, developing sensitive detection method for MP is of great significance to ensure environment and food safety.

During the past years, several analytical techniques have been designed to detect MP pesticide, such as gas chromatography, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry, chemiluminescence, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [7], electrochemical analysis [8] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [9]. Although the aforementioned methods are very important for detecting MP, their real applications are still restricted by some unavoidable shortcomings such as complex pre-treatment processes, high-cost and requiring skillful operation [10,11]. Recently, some interesting works have been reported for detecting MP based on the fluorescence nanoprobe due to its unique advantages on cost, convenience and sensitivity. For instance, Xu et al. [12] established a fluorescence sensor based on the catalytic reaction of organophosphorus hydrolase for MP detection. Huang et al. [13] construct a biosensor based on acetylcholinesterase enzymatic reaction for OPs assay. However, most of the developed approaches for MP detection based on fluorescence nanoprobe generally depend on the catalytic or inhibitory effect of the enzyme, and the enzyme is relatively expensive and easily inactivated with short service life. For resolving this problem in MP detection, there are a few sensor arrays based on enzyme-free fluorescent nanoprobe have been proposed most recently [14], but developing enzyme-free nanoprobe for the fluorescent detection of MP with high selectivity and sensitivity is still a challenge.

Transition metal chalcogenides attracted recently widespread attentions as a graphene-analogous two-dimensional nanomaterial which can exhibit uncommon and unique electronic, mechanical, optical, chemical and thermal properties [15,16]. Among these transition metal chalcogenides nanomaterials, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) has been exploited largely in the past years with important potential application as novel optical sensors, optoelectronic devices, batteries, transistors and catalysts. As is well-known, the optical properties from semiconductors relay mainly on the electronic band structure of nanomaterial [17]. When the indirect band gap from bulk MoS2 (about 1.2 eV) transfer to direct band gap of monolayer MoS2 (about 1.8 eV), the photoluminescent behavior from MoS2 can be aroused [18,19]. In addition, when the lateral size MoS2 nanosheets decrease to less than 10 nm to form MoS2 quantum dots (MoS2 QDs), the formed MoS2 QDs can show strong fluorescence owing to the quantum confinement and edge effects, and MoS2 QDs exhibit many unique superiorities such as low toxicity, environmentally friendly and easily functionalized [20]. Thus MoS2 QDs have been widely applied in fluorescence determination of metal ion [21], hyaluronidase, pyrophosphate [20,22], etc. [23]. However, most fluorescence sensing platforms based on pure MoS2 QDs usually exist several defects such as low selectivity and poor anti-interference [24], designing or bring in a new multifunctional molecule which has benign effects on the selectivity and sensitivity as well as other sensing performances of MoS2 QD is thus highly interesting and important technologically.

β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD), a cyclic oligosaccharide consisted of 7 glucose units, is a well-known molecular host which is capable of including various organic molecules inside its cavity to form host-guest inclusion complex [25]. Because of its hydrophobic inner cavity, hydrophilic surface structure and molecular recognition property, β-CD has been widely used to develop various sensing platforms via host-guest interaction [26]. Thus, it's believed that the combination of the strong fluorescence properties from MoS2 QD and high molecular recognition capabilities from β-CD can offer a novel pathway to construct highly sensitive and selective sensing platforms for different analytes including MP.

The previous studies demonstrated that MP can be hydrolyzed to p-nitrophenol (p-NP) under alkaline conditions [27], and p-NP can enter into the β-CD cavity via host-guest recognize between β-CD and p-NP [28]. Inspired by this insight, it's confirmed that the alliance of MoS2 QDs and β-CDs can develop selective and sensitive fluorescent nanoprobe for MP via measuring the signaling changes produced by the entrance of p-NP into β-CD cavity. In the present study, β-CD functionalized MoS2 QDs (β-CD-MoS2 QDs) was synthesized firstly and then used as a novel enzyme-free fluorescent nanoprobe for the detection of MP. As shown in Scheme S1, 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) modified MoS2 QDs was formed firstly via amidation reaction, which then couple with hydroxypropyl-β-CD to produce β-CD-MoS2 QDs. Under the alkaline conditions, the target MP can be hydrolyzed to p-NP and sequentially the formed p-NP enters into β-CD cavity via host-guest recognize between β-CD and p-NP, which results the fluorescence from β-CD-MoS2 QDs are effectively quenched (Scheme 1 ). Based on this principle, an effective and reliable sensing platform was constructed for MP. The experimental results demonstrated that the detection method based on β-CD-MoS2 QDs probe for MP has high selectivity and sensitivity as well as wide linear range and low detection limit. it's believed the developed proposal has important potential applications for MP analysis and the produced β-CD-MoS2 QDs nanoprobe has wide applications in the optical sensing.

Sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC·HCl), APBA, glutathione, p-NP, hydropropyl-β-CD, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), MP and other pesticides were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The fluorescence spectrums were measured with F-7000 Spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra were carried out on UV–Vis 2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was measured on a K-Alpha 1063 (Thermo Fisher, U. K.). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained using a JEM-3010 (Joel, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime was acquired by Horiba-Jobin-Yvon Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Japan). Purification of β-CD-MoS2 QDs was performed using a centrifuge 5804 R (Eppendorf, Germany).

MoS2 QDs were synthesized via a modified process reported in previous work [15]. Na2MoO4·2H2O (0.125 g) was dissolved in ultrapure water (12.5 mL) and adjusting its pH value to 6.5 with 0.1M HCl, then, the glutathione (0.215 g) and ultrapure water (25 mL) were added. After sonicating for about 10 min, the formed mixture solution was transferred into an autoclave (50.0 mL) and hydrothermally treated for one day at 200 °C. After cooled to room temperature, the large particles consisted of reaction solutions were removed through centrifugation. The pure MoS2 QDs solution was stored in 4 °C.

For synthesizing β-CD-MoS2 QDs, the produced above MoS2 QDs (20.0 mL) were added into 10.0 mL of ultrapure water, adjusting the pH value of the solution to 4.0. EDC·HCl (0.2 g) and NHS (0.2 g) were put into the MoS2 QDs solution and stirred continuously for 30 min. Next, APBA (0.25 g) was added into the solution and heated at 100 °C for 12 h. Adjusting the pH value of the resulting solution to 9.0 with NaOH solution, and adding 0.35 g hydroxypropyl-β-CD into the solution for functionalizing MoS2 QDs, the resulted mixed solution was heated continuously for 5 h at 100 °C. Finally, after purifying the obtained solution with 1000 Da dialysis bag for removing excessive EDC, NHS, APBA and β-CD, β-CD-MoS2 QDs was obtained successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of MP by β-CD-MoS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis of MP was performed through the following steps. At first, MP (100.0 μL) with a definite concentration was added into the solution of NaOH (35.0 mM) and incubated at 60 °C for 20 min to produce p-NP via hydrolytic action. Then, 10.0 μL of β-CD-MoS2 QDs was added into this mixed solution after adjusting its pH value to neutral with 5.0 mL PBS (pH 6.0). For achieving the fluorescence of MP, the fluorescence intensities at the excitation wavelength of 295 nm was measured.

After crushing the edible portion of the real samples, a total of 25.0 g of each sample was mixed with acetonitrile solution (50.0 mL) and then the mixture was filtered. The collected solution was dried on a water bath and subsequently dissolved in a methanol solution. Next, MP with various concentrations were added to the treated samples and incubating at 60 °C for 20 min with NaOH solution (35.0 mM) to produce p-NP via hydrolytic action. Then, the hydrolyzed samples were added to 5.0 mL PBS and mixed with β-CD-MoS2 QDs (10 μL), the fluorescence intensity was measured at the excitation wavelength of 295 nm.

The morphology and distribution of the obtained β-CD-MoS2 QDs were characterized by TEM, as shown in Fig. 1 A, the picture displayed the uniformly dispersed of β-CD-MoS2 QDs with an average diameter of ~3 nm, which is in consistent with that of original MoS2 QDs (Fig. S1). XPS spectrum was determined the surface element composition of β-CD-MoS2 QDs, and the result are shown in Fig. 1B. The binding energy signal at 395.2 eV corresponded to N–CO, which can confirmed the formation of an amide bond [29]. The peaks at 191.9 eV and 226.8 eV represented to B (-B-O-), and Mo, respectively [30,31]. Fig. S2A shows that the Mo 3d spectrum is deconvoluted into three peaks, which are located at 226.8 (S 2s), 228.0 (Mo 3d5/2) and 231.8 eV (Mo3d3/2), respectively [32,33]. From Fig. S2B, it can be found that the S 2p spectrum displayed S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 at 163 eV−1 and 164 eV−1, respectively, and the other two peaks in the spectrum are belong to S2O3 2− group [19]. In Fig. S2C, the B 1s spectrum shows two peaks appear at 191.0 eV-1 for B–C, 191.9 eV-1 for B–O [34,35]. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) of β-CD-MoS2 QDs was exhibited in Fig. S3, It's noted that the β-CD-MoS2 QDs shows strong peaks of MoS2 QDs at 3400 cm−1, 1640 cm−1 and 1036 cm−1 ascribed to N–H stretching vibration, N–H bending vibration and B–O–H vibration, respectively. Meanwhile, the typical absorption features of β-CD for the coupled C–O–C stretching/O–H bending vibrations at 1146 cm−1 and CH2 stretching vibrations at 2915 cm−1 can also be observed in β-CD-MoS2 QDs. The results indicate that β-CD molecules were successfully linked covalently to MoS2 QDs to produce β-CD- MoS2 QDs nanoprobe through phenylboronic acid and amide bond.

The UV–vis displayed the different absorption spectrum between β-CD-MoS2 QDs and MoS2 QDs. The β-CD-MoS2 QDs shows two remarkable absorption peaks at 238 nm and 289 nm, where no absorption peaks can be found for MoS2 QDs (Fig. S4) [30]. Moreover, Fig. 2 reveals that the excitation and emission peaks of β-CD-MoS2 QDs are greatly shifted compared to that of MoS2 QDs, suggesting that the β-CD covalently link to the surface of APBA functionalized MoS2 QDs through coupled effect between boronic acids and cis-diols. In summary, by comparing the XPS, UV–vis and fluorescence spectrum of β-CD-MoS2 QDs and MoS2 QDs, it's ensured that the β-CD-MoS2 QDs fluorescent nanoprobe was successfully synthesized.

To further explore the stability of the as-prepared fluorescent nanoprobe, the fluorescence intensity of β-CD-MoS2 QDs exposed to daylight for 8 h and in different concentrations of NaCl solution was measured. As illustrated in Fig. S5, no significant changes observed in the fluorescence intensity of β-CD-MoS2 QDs under irradiation of daylight for 8 h or in a highly concentrated solution of NaCl. These results illustrate the synthesized β-CD-MoS2 QDs possess excellent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PET process between β-CD-MoS2 QDs and p-NP
ce:section.ce:para: According to the previous study [36], p-NP is capable to enter the β-CD cavity to form a host-guest inclusion because of the large binding constant (2.3 × 103 M−1) [37]. This process can close the distance between p-NP and β-CD-MoS2 QDs, and can quench the fluorescence of β-CD-MoS2 QDs. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity of β-CD-MoS2 QDs dramatically decreased with the addition of p-NP, which was consistent with resonance energy transfer (RET) or photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process [38]. For distinguishing whether the quenching effect is resulted from RET or PET, the fluorescence lifetime decay curve was further measured. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence lifetime of β-CD-MoS2 QDs shows significant shorter after the addition of p-NP, which certificated that the quenching mechanism is related to PET [39]. Moreover, the absorption spectrum from p-NP and emission spectrum from β-CD-MoS2 QDs verifies the hypothesis, as from Fig. S6, almost no overlap presents between the emission spectrum from β-CD-MoS2 QDs and absorption spectrum from p-NP, which is inconsistent with the resonance energy transfer (RET) mechanism [40]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of p-NP for β-CD-MoS2 QDs is attributed to PET process. Furthermore, an obviously blue-shift in fluorescence of β-CD-MoS2 QDs is observed in the presence of p-NP. The blue shift might be attribute to the influence via host-guest interaction between p-NP and β-CD on the optical property of nanoprobe [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of MP based on β-CD-MoS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the feature that MP can be hydrolyzed to produce p-NP under alkaline condition, and then the resulting p-NP can quench efficiently the fluorescence of β-CD-MoS2 QDs, the fluorescence detection of MP was carried out based on the β-CD-MoS2 QDs nanoprobe. Firstly, the feasibility of the proposal was studied and the related results are exhibited in Fig. 4 . For pure MoS2 QDs (Fig. 4A), its fluorescence intensity only shows a very slight decrease in the presence of hydrolyzed MP. As for β-CD-MoS2 QDs (Fig. 4B), its fluorescence intensity barely changed in the presence or absence of MP; however, it can be dramatically quenched in the present of hydrolyzed MP, suggesting the produced p-NP from the hydrolysis reaction of MP can entry into the β-CD cavity to quench the fluorescence intensity of β-CD-MoS2 QDs. These results demonstrate that the fluorescence detection of MP based on β-CD-MoS2 QDs is feasible.

To realize the best performance of β-CD-MoS2 QDs for detecting MP, some experiment conditions were optimized. As illustrated in Fig. S7A, the absorbance intensity of MP at 400 nm reached the highest after the addition of 35 mM NaOH. However, as exhibited in Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity of β-CD-MoS2 QDs is dramatically decreased when the solution has a strongly alkaline (pH = 10.0). This phenomenon might be due to the broken of amide bond, and the β-CD molecules cannot connect with MoS2 QDs. Thus, the pH of hydrolyzed MP solution was adjusted to neutral. Furthermore, the optimum for incubation time and temperature were also studied. As shown in Fig. S7B, the (F0–F)/F0 (F0 represents the background fluorescence of β-CD-MoS2 without MP, and F represents the fluorescence of β-CD-MoS2 with different MP concentrations) reaches a maximum when the reaction time reached 20 min. It is noted that the quenching efficiency reaches a maximum when the temperature of the system is 60 °C (Fig. S7C). Based on these observations, the analyte system was incubated at 70 °C for 20 min before the measurement for further experiments.

Under the optimum conditions, the usability of this fluorescent nanoprobe for sensitive detection of MP was measured. Fig. 5 A shows the fluorescence intensity of β-CD-MoS2 QDs gradually decreases with MP concentrations increasing from 0.01 ppm to 36.0 ppm. It can be observed a good linear relationship is presented between the fluorescence intensity and concentrations of MP ranging from 0.01 ppm to 18.0 ppm (Fig. 5B). The regression equation can be expressed as (F0–F)/F0 = 0.059[MP]+0.085, where R2 is 0.992. The detection limit was estimated to be 3.3 ppb (S/N = 3). A comparison to previous methods for MP detection is shown in Table S1, which suggests that the β-CD-MoS2 QDs based assay exhibits lower detection limit and wider linear range. More importantly, the proposed method is the first example to achieve sensitive detection of MP using a β-CD modified MoS2 QDs through host-guest recognition without the addition of enzyme.

To validate the selectivity of the proposed proposal for the determination of MP, the other organophosphorus pesticides (triazophos, dibrom, phosphamidon, glyphosate, methamidophos, dimethoate, phoxim, systox, and trichlorfon) were also analyzed using the β-CD-MoS2 QDs fluorescent nanoprobe. As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of β-CD-MoS2 QDs by MP (20.0 ppm) is prominent higher than other pesticides with relatively higher concentration (100.0 ppm). These results demonstrate that the proposed method is highly selective for distinguishing MP from other organophosphorus pesticides. Next, the practicality of the fluorescence nanoprobe for MP detection was investigated in real sample analysis including fruit and vegetable. Different concentration of MP (0.0, 1.0, 5.0 and 10.0 ppm) were spiked with processed apple and cucumber samples, the fluorescence was measured in a similar experiment procedure, and the results exhibit good recoveries ranging from 93.0% to 105.6% with a maximum RSD of 4.42% (Table S2), indicating that the proposed fluorescent nanoprobe has important potential applicability for the determination of MP in real samples.

In summary, this work developed an enzyme-free fluorescent nanoprobe based on β-CD-MoS2 QDs for MP detection through host-guest recognize for the first time. By introducing β-CD molecules on the MoS2 QDs surface, the formed novel β-CD-MoS2 QDs nanoprobe can exhibit the synthetic advantages from each component (e.g., excellent optical property of MoS2 QDs and high molecular recognition performance of β-CD). Via the PET process, the p-NP molecules from MP hydrolysis under alkaline conditions can entry into the inner cavities of β-CD owing to its host-guest recognition and further quench the fluorescence of β-CD-MoS2 QDs, thus a fluorescence sensing platform was constructed for MP. After optimizing various experimental conditions, the results show that the β-CD-MoS2 QDs fluorescent nanoprobe presents excellent selectivity and sensitivity with wide linear range (three orders of magnitude) and low detection limit (3.3 ppb) toward MP detection. Furthermore, the developed nanoprobe can be applied successfully to the real food samples for MP analysis. It's believed that the β-CD-MoS2 QDs probe can give a new sight for the selective and sensitive detection of MP and has potential for chem/biological analysis.

Yinhui Yi: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Experiment, Writing- Reviewing and Editing. Wei Zeng: Experiment, Data curation, Writing-Editing. Gangbing Zhu: Validation, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21607061), the CAS Key Laboratory of Coastal Environmental Processes and Ecological Remediation, YICCAS (Grant No.: 2020KFJJ16), the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics of Hunan University (2018019), the Open Project Program of Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology, Ministry of Education (No. FS2008) and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions, Collaborative Innovation Center of Technology and Material of Water Treatment.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121703.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of extraction, analytical and advanced methods for determination of pesticides in environment and foodstuffs
Trends Food Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Samsidar, S. Siddiquee, S. M. Shaarani, A review of extraction, analytical and advanced methods for determination of pesticides in environment and foodstuffs, Trends in Food Science & Technology 71 (2018) 188-201.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dots applied to methodology on detection of pesticide and veterinary drug residues
J. Agric. Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.-W. Zhou, X.-M. Zou, S.-H. Song, G.-H. Chen, Quantum dots applied to methodology on detection of pesticide and veterinary drug residues, Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry 66 (2018) 1307-1319..

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Shell thickness-dependent Raman enhancement for rapid identification and detection of pesticide residues at fruit peels
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Liu, G. Han, Z. Zhang, R. Liu, C. Jiang, S. Wang, M.-Y. Han, Shell thickness-dependent raman enhancement for rapid identification and detection of pesticide residues at fruit peels, Analytical Chemistry 84 (2012) 255-261.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile green synthesis of graphene-Au nanorod nanoassembly for on-line extraction and sensitive stripping analysis of methyl parathion
Electrochim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Zhu, W. Liu, T. Li, X. Yue, T. Liu, W. Zhang, S. Yu, D. Zhang, J. Wang, Facile green synthesis of graphene-Au nanorod nanoassembly for on-line extraction and sensitive stripping analysis of methyl parathion, Electrochimica Acta 146 (2014) 419-428.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient abiotic assay formats for methyl parathion: molecularly imprinted polymer nanoparticle assay as an alternative to enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Esen, J. Czulak, T. Cowen, E. Piletska, S. A. Piletsky, Highly efficient abiotic assay formats for methyl parathion: molecularly imprinted polymer nanoparticle assay as an alternative to enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, Analytical Chemistry 91 (2019) 958-964.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent sensor for indirect measurement of methyl parathion based on alkaline-induced hydrolysis using N-doped carbon dots
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Lan, X. Wang, Q. Liu, J. Bao, M. Yang, H. Fa, C. Hou, D. Huo, Fluorescent sensor for indirect measurement of methyl parathion based on alkaline-induced hydrolysis using N-doped carbon dots, Talanta 192 (2019) 368-373.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flexible bipyramid-AuNPs based SERS tape sensing strategy for detecting methyl parathion on vegetable and fruit surface
Sensor. Actuator. B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wu, Y. Luo, C. Hou, D. Huo, Y. Zhou, S. Zou, J. Zhao, Y. Lei, Flexible bipyramid-AuNPs based SERS tape sensing strategy for detecting methyl parathion on vegetable and fruit surface, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical 285 (2019) 123-128.
